lessonTitle: Acute Chest Syndrome in Sickle Cell Disease

items.html: Define acute chest syndrome (ACS) in sickle cell disease (SCD)
Identify diagnostic indicators of ACS
Recognize the different risk factors for the development of ACS
Identify best practices in the management of ACS

data.html:
A 5-year-old girl with Hb SS had leg pains and chest/abdominal pain. While in the hospital, she developed a fever of 38.9°C. Her chest X-ray (CXR) was within normal limits (WNL). Nasopharyngeal swab (NPS) tested positive for parainfluenza.

Initially, she was managed with intravenous (IV) hydration at 1.5 times the normal maintenance rate and IV morphine. Over the course of a couple of days, she experienced increased work of breathing (WOB), leading to the activation of the PACE (Pediatric Acute Care Evaluation) rapid response team.

She was transferred to PICU (Pediatric Intensive Care Unit) due to hypoxia (oxygen saturation SpO2 80-84%).

Chest X-ray (CXR) showed worsening of lung infiltrates on both sides.

Complete blood count (CBC) revealed a hemoglobin (Hb) level of 69, a white blood cell count (WBC) of 31.5 (with an ANC, or absolute neutrophil count, of 23.6), and a platelet count of 236.

Blood gas analysis showed a pH of 7.27, bicarbonate (HCO3) level of 20, pO2 (partial pressure of oxygen) of 36, and pCO2 (partial pressure of carbon dioxide) of 44.

A diagnosis of acute chest crisis was made.

Treatment involved receiving continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) and two packed red cell transfusions.

slides.title: What are your current differential diagnoses for this patient?

data.question: What are your current differential diagnoses for this patient?
data.feedback_correct: All of these could be part of your differentials, except for Asthma Exacerbation. Learn about Acute Chest Syndrome on the next slide.

data.html: ACS is a medical emergency! Don't hesitate to reach out to Haematology for consultation if you are ever unsure.
Chest infiltrates may lag behind clinical symptoms by up to 3 days.
ACS is a type of acute lung injury that can lead to increased mortality and death. Learn more by tapping on the images below to zoom in.

images.alt: Acute Chest Syndrome in children with sickle cell disease: Current perspectives on pathogenesis and treatment. Photo credit: Uwaezuoke, S. N., 2022

slides.title: Etiology or Associated Conditions With ACS

data.question: Which of the following pathogens is most commonly associated with infection-related ACS?
data.feedback_correct: Infection-related ACS can involve various pathogens, including Chlamydia, Mycoplasma Pneumonia, Staphylococcus Aureus, and Streptococcus Pneumoniae.

slides.title: Risk Factors for the Development of ACS

items.html: Young age
SCD genotype Hb SS and Sβ0 > Hb SC and Hb Sβ+
Lower concentration of Hb F (increase in HbF from 5-15% reduces the risk by half)
Higher steady state Hb level
Higher steady state RBC count
Prior ACS (esp. < 3 yrs of age)
Greater than 3 severe VOC episodes
History of asthma/airway hyperreactivity
Smoking
Recent trauma or surgery

slides.title: In Which Age Group Is ACS Most Commonly Associated With Infection?

data.html:
In which age group is ACS most commonly associated with infection?

Adolescents (10-19 years)

Adolescents in the age group of 10-19 years have a higher association of ACS with infection.

data.question: Besides removing sickle cells, what one other benefit does exchange transfusions offer in sickle cell disease?
data.feedback_correct: Exchange transfusions help to reduce hemolysis in individuals with sickle cell disease, which is important for overall health.

items.html: ACS is the leading cause of death in Sickle Cell Disease (SCD), responsible for around 25% of SCD-related deaths.

To be diagnosed with ACS, a patient must meet the following criteria: new pulmonary infiltrates on chest imaging (chest x-ray, CT) involving at least one lung segment; chest pain; fever; tachypnea, wheezing, rales, cough; hypoxemia; increased work of breathing
Acute management includes: rapid identification, treatment with oxygen, hydration, pain medications, transfusions, antibiotics, and incentive spirometry

Vichinsky EP, Neumayr LD, Earles AN, Williams R, Lennette ET, Dean D, Nickerson B, Orringer E, McKie V, Bellevue R, Daeschner C, Manci EA. Causes and outcomes of the acute chest syndrome in sickle cell disease. National Acute Chest Syndrome Study Group. N Engl J Med. 2000 Jun 22;342(25):1855-65. doi: 10.1056/NEJM200006223422502. https://emcrit.org/ibcc/sickle-chest/ (Accessed February 4, 2024)
